FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 2 (2018–2020), N = 10,258/11,875, 21 recruitment sites across the United States

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using modified Poisson regression examining associations between cyberbullying and eating disorder symptoms

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in International Journal of Eating Disorders, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying alone without separately-measured smartphone or social media variable. Paper examines cyberbullying victimization/perpetration (online, texts, group texts, social media contexts) paired with eating disorder symptoms. Per criteria §2.4, cyberbullying without isolated SM/phone measurement does not qualify; the SM context is part of the bullying definition, not a standalone SM/phone variable as IV or DV

notes: Paper fails FT-IC2 because it measures cyberbullying (a construct that occurs online/via texts/social media) as the independent variable, not a distinct smartphone or social media use measure. No separate phone/SM time or usage variable is operationalized alongside or in relation to eating disorder symptoms.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract states follow-up from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study; references mixed-effects models for prospective analysis of associations between baseline screen time and mental health

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using mixed-effects models; prospective design from baseline to follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: No PDF available. Abstract mentions self-reported screen time as independent variable and mental health as outcome. However, abstract does not specify whether screen time is isolated phone/SM (qualifies) or multi-device aggregate (TV + console + phone with no breakdown; does not qualify per §2.4). Default rule in §2.1 insufficient to resolve FT-IC2 without PDF text

notes: Paper requires PDF to determine if screen time variable isolates phone/SM use. Without methods section, cannot confirm whether this is a phone/SM-specific measure or a multi-device composite without breakdown.

FT_IC1.evidence: Study describes participants from the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) cohort study. This is Dutch ABCD (Amsterdam), not the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. Per criteria §2.1, ABCD cohorts in other countries (including Amsterdam) are explicitly listed as NOT_MET

notes: Paper uses Amsterdam Born Children and their Development cohort, which is a non-U.S. ABCD study (Dutch population). Fails FT-IC1 cohort criterion immediately; all downstream criteria deferred per cascade convention.
